metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for AC2, e.g. a unit test that reads a new-format event with an older schema-version reader (or strips the new fields and asserts the record still round-trips cleanly), and state the expected pass condition.
findings.finding: AC2 ('the added author fields are confirmed additive') has no concrete proving command — 'confirmed' is process-framed with no stated test artifact, command, or observable output.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, second bullet: 'The added author fields are confirmed additive (an older reader ignores them; no store-format break).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Disambiguate the two approaches: specify that the implementation should stamp `attribution_fields()` + `_apply_authorship()` INLINE inside `_write_event_file` (not route through `_seam.append_event`), because `_write_event_file` is deliberately uncommitted and routing through `append_event`/`write_and_push` would break the reconciler's batch-commit model.
findings.finding: The plan's two approaches — 'route through _seam.append_event' vs 'stamp attribution + sign inline' — are presented as equivalent but are NOT: _seam.append_event calls write_and_push() which commits+pushes per event, while _write_event_file is explicitly designed to leave files uncommitted for the reconciler's own batch commit orchestration (per the module docstring and the workflow's separate 'Commit reconciler events back' step). Naively routing inbound writes through _seam.append_event would produce one git commit per inbound event, bypassing the reconciler's batch commit model and potentially conflicting with the reconciler's own orchestration.
findings.location: Plan body — 'Route inbound event writes through the signing seam (or stamp attribution + sign inline)'; src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_translate.py module docstring
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the parenthetical 'or stamp attribution + sign inline' with a single directed approach: 'Modify `_write_event_file` in `inbound_translate.py` to call `_seam.attribution_fields(repo_root)` and `_seam._apply_authorship(event, ...)` after composing the event dict, mirroring the pattern in `_seam.append_event` (lines 374–380) but before the `write_lock` block — keeping the file uncommitted for the reconciler's own orchestrated commit.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a prerequisite step to the plan: a Rebar Bot identity ticket with `email: joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` must exist in the store (via `rebar identity create`) before signing can resolve `author_id`; document how to bootstrap it and how its ticket-id is referenced.
findings.finding: The email-to-identity resolution chain requires a pre-existing identity ticket in the store with `email == joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`. The plan directs setting `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` to that address so 'git-email resolution maps to the bot identity', but never specifies that a Rebar Bot identity ticket must be provisioned in the store with that email. Without a matching identity ticket, `resolve_current_identity()` returns `None`, `author_id` is absent from the event envelope, and `_apply_authorship()` cannot sign (its guard is `if author_id and signing_key`), leaving events `unsigned`/`unknown-author` — exactly the problem the plan intends to fix.
findings.location: Plan body — 'set BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL=joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com so git-email resolution maps to the bot identity'; src/rebar/_commands/identity.py `_match_by_email` / `resolve_current_identity`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a positive rationale for the chosen implementation approach (inline stamping inside `_write_event_file`) — explain WHY it fits (preserves uncommitted-file model, minimal blast radius, mirrors existing `append_event` pattern) — and remove the 'or equivalently' fork.
findings.finding: The plan presents two mutually exclusive implementation paths ('route through the signing seam' / 'stamp attribution + sign inline') without stating a positive rationale for the chosen approach, leaving the implementer to make a design decision that the plan should resolve.
findings.location: Plan body — 'Route inbound event writes through the signing seam (or stamp attribution + sign inline)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (and add a plan step for) the prerequisite that a bot identity ticket with email `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` exists in the store before relying on git-email resolution to produce `author_id` for signing.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that setting BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL=joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com 'maps to the bot identity,' but resolve_current_identity() only produces an author_id (required for signing) when an identity ticket with that exact email already exists in the tickets store. This is an unverified capability assertion: if no such ticket exists, events will carry author_email but no author_id, and _apply_authorship will silently skip signing.
findings.location: Plan body — 'set BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL=joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com (currently rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com) so git-email resolution maps to the bot identity'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit plan step: 'Ensure a bot identity ticket (`rebar identity create --name "RebarBotNava" --email joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com --key <pub> --self`) exists in the store before deploying, OR use `rebar identity use <bot-id>` (pointer file) in the GHA step to bypass email resolution entirely.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a plan step (or AC) to create/seed the Rebar Bot identity ticket (`rebar identity create --name 'rebar-bridge[bot]' --email joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com --self`) in the store BEFORE the signing seam is activated, so `resolve_current_identity()` can match the bot's git email and populate `author_id`.
findings.finding: The signing path in `_seam.append_event` activates only when `resolve_current_identity()` returns a non-None `author_id`, which requires an identity ticket in the store whose `email` field matches the bot's `git config user.email`. The plan sets `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL=joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` in git config but never mentions creating a Rebar Bot identity ticket with that email. Without this ticket, `_match_by_email` returns `None`, `attribution_fields()` omits `author_id`, `_seam.py:294` skips signing, and reconciler events classify as `unknown-author` — the exact failure the plan is meant to fix, still present after the code change.
findings.location: Plan body (signing seam section) + `src/rebar/_commands/_seam.py:293-294` + `src/rebar/_commands/identity.py:530-544`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-time bootstrap step — either in the workflow as a `rebar identity create ...` invocation guarded by 'only if not already present', or as a manual prerequisite documented in `docs/jira-sync-setup.md` — that creates the bot's identity ticket with `email=joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`. Then optionally use `rebar identity use <bot-id>` to set the pointer, making resolution order-independent of email matching.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the key-file path (e.g., `$RUNNER_TEMP/signing.key`), the exact creation mechanism (GHA `run` step or dedicated action), and add an explicit `if: always()` cleanup step to delete the file after the job.
findings.finding: The job-local key file's lifecycle is under-specified: the plan says to inject the secret to a file but never specifies the file path, how it is created (a `run` step writing to `$RUNNER_TEMP`, a GHA secret-file action, etc.), or how/whether it is cleaned up after the job — leaving a key-leak risk on self-hosted or persistent runners.
findings.location: reconcile-bridge.yml — injection step; AC line 'key is never logged/committed'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a workflow step: `echo "${{ secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY }}" > "$RUNNER_TEMP/rebar_signing.key"` and a final step with `if: always()` that runs `rm -f "$RUNNER_TEMP/rebar_signing.key"`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete key-injection controls in the plan: use `$RUNNER_TEMP` as the file path, `chmod 600` immediately after writing, optionally a GHA `add-mask` step for the key bytes, and an `if: always()` cleanup step that `shred`/`rm`s the file — so AC3's 'never logged/committed' constraint is mechanically enforced, not just stated.
findings.finding: The plan directs injection of `secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY` to a job-local file but specifies no concrete controls to prevent the key material from leaking: it names no protective file path (e.g., `$RUNNER_TEMP`), no GHA log-masking step (`echo '::add-mask::...'`), no file permission hardening (`chmod 600`), and no post-job cleanup step. AC3's 'the key is never logged/committed' is an intent statement, not a mechanism.
findings.location: Plan body: 'inject `secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY` to a job-local file' / AC3: 'the key is never logged/committed'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a dedicated plan step: (1) write the secret to `$RUNNER_TEMP/rebar_signing.key` (ephemeral, runner-scoped), (2) `chmod 600 $RUNNER_TEMP/rebar_signing.key`, (3) point `identity.signing_key` at that path in rebar config or env, (4) add an `if: always()` post-step that removes the file. This satisfies the GHA secret-file handling best practice and makes AC3 mechanically verifiable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace file-based key injection with the existing `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` env-var mechanism (injected directly via `env: REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY: ${{ secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY }}`), which GitHub Actions auto-masks in logs; if file injection is retained, specify `$RUNNER_TEMP`, `chmod 0600`, `::add-mask::` registration of the value, and explicit post-step cleanup in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan instructs injecting `secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY` to a 'job-local file' but specifies no concrete secret-lifecycle controls (file permissions, secure temp path, ::add-mask:: registration, or post-step cleanup), leaving the private key material potentially exposed in logs or on the runner filesystem. The codebase already supports REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY as an env-var override (src/rebar/_config_schema.py:314 comment; evidenced by tests), which GHA's built-in secrets masking handles safely without file materialization.
findings.location: Plan body: 'inject `secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY` to a job-local file, set `identity.signing_key` to it' / AC3: 'the key is never logged/committed'
findings.suggested_fix: In reconcile-bridge.yml's 'Run reconciler' step env block, add `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY: ${{ secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY }}` directly (no file needed — the existing env-var override in _config_schema.py will pick it up). Remove the file-injection instruction from the plan. Update AC3 to reference this mechanism explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or explicit note) specifying the reconciler's behavior when the signing key is absent or invalid: it must fail loudly (non-zero exit / logged error) rather than silently falling back to unsigned events.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion or test plan addresses the failure path when `REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY` is missing, malformed, or mismatched — a new failure mode introduced by routing writes through the signing seam.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all three items)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a live/end-to-end acceptance criterion requiring an actual reconcile-bridge GHA workflow run (or a dedicated CI job) to produce at least one event that passes `rebar verify-identity` in the CI environment, confirming key injection and email resolution work end-to-end.
findings.finding: AC1 ('a synced event verifies as `verified`') is satisfiable offline without ever running the GHA reconcile-bridge workflow, leaving the critical cutover path — key injection at CI runtime, correct email resolution, and signing in the GHA environment — unvalidated. A live/CI-execution criterion is absent.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC1 / `.github/workflows/reconcile-bridge.yml` section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'Verified by a passing CI run of `reconcile-bridge.yml` that produces a synced event; the event's `author_sig` is validated by `rebar verify-identity` in the same or a downstream workflow step, with the result logged as a job output.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a step (or sub-command) describing how to produce a test synced event to populate the store before running `rebar verify-identity` — e.g. trigger the reconcile workflow on a test branch, or run a local fixture replay — so AC1's proving command has a non-empty target.
findings.finding: No step describes how to produce the synced event artifact needed to exercise AC1's proving command (`rebar verify-identity`), creating a missing prerequisite-state ordering gap.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, first bullet: 'a synced event verifies as `verified` under `rebar verify-identity`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for the 'never logged/committed' sub-claim in AC3, e.g. `grep -r 'REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY' .github/workflows/reconcile-bridge.yml | grep -v 'secrets\.'` returning empty, plus confirm the workflow has no `echo`/`run` step that expands the secret.
findings.finding: AC3 ('the key is never logged/committed') has no concrete proving command; this is a security property with no in-session check named.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, third bullet: '…the key is never logged/committed.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'Verified by: `grep -rn "$REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY\|signing_key" workflow-run-log.txt` returns no matches; the key file is written to a `$RUNNER_TEMP` path and the step is not `echo`-ed anywhere in the workflow YAML.'

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve and state the single chosen implementation approach (seam routing vs inline stamping) so the executor has no design fork to resolve.
findings.finding: The plan presents two mutually exclusive implementation approaches ('route through the signing seam' vs 'stamp attribution + sign inline') without resolving which to use, leaving a real architectural design decision to the executor.
findings.location: Body bullet 1: 'Route inbound event writes through the signing seam (or stamp attribution + sign inline)'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the parenthetical alternative and commit to one path, e.g.: 'Route inbound event writes through `_seam.append_event` so the seam remains the single write choke-point.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `.github/workflows/reconcile-bridge-canary.yml` to the list of files requiring the `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` update (line 100 hardcodes the same default email as `reconcile-bridge.yml`).
findings.finding: `reconcile-bridge-canary.yml` also hardcodes `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL: ${{ vars.BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL || 'rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com' }}` (line 100) — the same email default the plan changes to `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` — but the plan names only `reconcile-bridge.yml` for the email update, leaving the canary workflow as an unenumerated consumer.
findings.location: Plan body — 'In `.github/workflows/reconcile-bridge.yml`: ... set `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL=joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`'; `.github/workflows/reconcile-bridge-canary.yml:100`
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the plan's workflow edit target to include `reconcile-bridge-canary.yml:100`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit acknowledgement of the committer-email identity switch (`rebar-bridge@...` → `joeoakhart+bot@...`) as a breaking change for email-keyed consumers, and specify a rollback or expand-contract path (e.g. a grace-period alias, a documented cut-over date, or a revert step) in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan changes `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` from `rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com` to `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` without acknowledging this as a breaking change for any downstream consumers (CI filters, audit scripts, notification rules, or identity-resolution logic) that key off the old committer email, and provides no rollback or expand-contract path for the workflow change.
findings.location: Plan body — GHA step: 'set `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL=joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` (currently `rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short 'Migration / Rollback' note to the plan body: list any known consumers of `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` / git committer email, document that the change is a hard cut-over (no alias), and describe the revert procedure (e.g. restore the old env-var value in the workflow and re-run) if the new identity fails to resolve.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or prose) specifying the error-handling contract when signing fails or the key is absent: fail-fast with a clear error log (preferred) vs. degrade gracefully, and confirm no partial/corrupt writes occur.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new failure point (signing seam / key injection) in the reconciler write path but specifies no error-handling behavior: what happens if the signing key is absent, malformed, or the seam throws — does the write proceed unsigned, fail the run, or silently drop the event?
findings.location: CRITICAL section / Acceptance Criteria (all three ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'If REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY is absent or signing raises, the reconciler job fails with a non-zero exit and a logged error; no unsigned event is written to the store.' Guard the seam call with a try/except that re-raises after logging, and add a pre-flight key-presence check at job startup.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note (or AC line) confirming that routing through `_seam.append_event` introduces no unintended side-effects or unmet preconditions in the reconciler's runtime context, or specify an 'inline stamping' fallback if the seam is unavailable.
findings.finding: The plan changes the call path from `rebar._store.event_append` to `_seam.append_event` but does not acknowledge whether `_seam.append_event` introduces new preconditions (seam initialization, config presence) or side-effects (notifications, hooks) that could affect other callers or break the reconciler in environments where the seam is not fully initialized.
findings.location: inbound_translate.py `_write_event_file`; plan body 'Route inbound event writes through the signing seam'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the fix is applied in `_write_event_file`'s body (covering all ~13 callsites across `apply_inbound.py` and `apply_inbound_records.py` automatically) and update the plan's file path reference from `_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_translate.py` to `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_translate.py`.
findings.finding: The plan names the implementation file as `_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_translate.py` but the actual path is `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_translate.py`; additionally, `_write_event_file` is defined in `inbound_translate.py` but called from `apply_inbound.py` (6 callsites) and `apply_inbound_records.py` (7 callsites) — the plan's scope description implies the fix is bounded to `inbound_translate.py` without acknowledging these callers.
findings.location: Plan body: '`_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_translate.py` `_write_event_file`, ~214-259'
findings.suggested_fix: State explicitly: 'Stamp `author_id`/`author_email`/`author_sig` inside `_write_event_file` in `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_translate.py` (the single definition point), so all 13+ callsites across `apply_inbound.py` and `apply_inbound_records.py` inherit the fix automatically.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/jira-sync-setup.md` (lines 96 and 119) to the edit set to update the documented `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` default/example from `rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com` to `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`.
findings.finding: `docs/jira-sync-setup.md` documents `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` with the old default value `rebar-bridge@users.noreply.github.com` at lines 96 and 119 (setup instructions and configuration table), but the plan does not enumerate this doc as requiring an update when the email changes to `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`.
findings.location: Plan body — email change to `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`; `docs/jira-sync-setup.md:96,119`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a documentation step (CLAUDE.md note or brief ADR) capturing the decision: 'all reconciler inbound writes must route through _seam.append_event to carry bot-identity signing; direct event_append calls are forbidden in this path.'
findings.finding: The plan introduces the invariant 'all reconciler-inbound writes must be signed by the bot identity' as a new architectural contract but includes no documentation update (ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design doc) capturing this decision for future maintainers.
findings.location: Plan body / Acceptance Criteria

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or implementation note requiring that the signing step emits an observable signal (e.g., a structured log line with author_id and sig presence, or a GHA step summary) so failures are visible without manual audits.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify observability for the new signing step: no log, metric, or alert is called out to confirm signing succeeded or to surface failures in production runs.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria / reconcile-bridge.yml section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a GHA `concurrency:` group (e.g., `concurrency: reconcile-bridge`) to serialize runs, or confirm that the key-file path is unique per job (e.g., keyed on `${{ github.run_id }}`) and that `event_append` is safe under concurrent calls.
findings.finding: Concurrency safety is unaddressed: if two reconcile-bridge GHA jobs run simultaneously (e.g., a scheduled run overlapping a manual re-run), both jobs could write to the same key-file path and race on `identity.signing_key` config, and the plan does not specify whether `event_append` (or `_seam.append_event`) is safe under concurrent writers.
findings.location: reconcile-bridge.yml; inbound_translate.py `_write_event_file`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'set identity.signing_key to it' with 'expose the secret as `REBAR_SIGNING_KEY` env var on the Run reconciler step' — this is the existing, tested, documented injection mechanism and requires no temp file.
findings.finding: The plan says 'set identity.signing_key to it' (the injected secret), but identity.signing_key expects a *file path* to a key on disk, not the key value itself. The existing, documented, and CI-tested injection mechanism for key values is the REBAR_SIGNING_KEY env var (signing.py line 98: `os.environ.get('REBAR_SIGNING_KEY')`), which already handles stripping/encoding. Wiring the secret through identity.signing_key would require writing the key to a temp file first, adding unnecessary steps and a novel config path that duplicates what REBAR_SIGNING_KEY already does.
findings.location: Plan body: 'inject secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY to a job-local file, set identity.signing_key to it'
findings.suggested_fix: In reconcile-bridge.yml, add `REBAR_SIGNING_KEY: ${{ secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY }}` to the 'Run reconciler' step's `env:` block. Remove the 'inject to a job-local file, set identity.signing_key' language from the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/mutate/test_dependency_direction.py` as a consumer that needs updating (or confirm it is unaffected) based on whichever implementation path is chosen for routing inbound writes through the signing logic.
findings.finding: The plan's first implementation option ('route inbound event writes through the signing seam / `_seam.append_event`') would require importing `rebar._commands._seam` from within the reconciler package, but `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/mutate/test_dependency_direction.py` statically enforces that reconciler modules may only import two engine seams (`ticket_reducer` and `event_append`). This guard test is an unenumerated consumer that the plan must either update (to permit the new import) or acknowledge by choosing the inline-stamp alternative.
findings.location: Plan body — 'Route inbound event writes through the signing seam (or stamp attribution + sign inline)'; `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/mutate/test_dependency_direction.py:33`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the plan: if routing through `_seam.append_event`, update `ALLOWED` in `test_dependency_direction.py` to include the new import (or use an internal helper); if stamping inline in `_write_event_file`, the guard test is unaffected.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` env var (the existing documented override for `identity.signing_key`) is sufficient for the GHA step before committing to the write-to-file approach; prefer the env var if it satisfies the need.
findings.finding: The plan proposes 'inject secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY to a job-local file, set identity.signing_key to it' without considering the already-existing REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY environment-variable override, which is the established, documented mechanism for CI key injection and avoids writing key material to disk at all.
findings.location: Plan body — 'In `.github/workflows/reconcile-bridge.yml`: inject `secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY` to a job-local file, set `identity.signing_key` to it'
findings.suggested_fix: In the reconcile-bridge.yml step, set `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY: ${{ secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY }}` directly in the `env:` block of the 'Run reconciler' step instead of writing to a file — the config layer already picks this up.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a post-rollback state assertion to the plan: explicitly state that rolling back the signing code leaves the store in a valid mixed state (some events signed, historical events unsigned), that this is safe, and that unsigned historical reconciler events are the expected pre-feature baseline — not a regression detectable by `rebar verify-identity`.
findings.finding: The plan does not assert on the store's schema state after a code rollback. Per the rubric, 'rollback exits 0' is insufficient — the plan must confirm what state the store is in after rollback. Here, after rolling back the signing code, future events will be unsigned again, but already-signed events remain on disk. The plan never states this explicitly, leaving ambiguity about whether the store is in a consistent or mixed state post-rollback.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 / plan body 'additive' claim
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2 or a new AC: 'After a code rollback, the store is in a known-good mixed state: events written before the fix remain unsigned (pre-feature baseline), events written after the fix carry full attribution, and all are readable by both old and new readers. `rebar verify-identity` treats unsigned reconciler events as pre-feature baseline, not a regression.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly declare in the plan whether a backfill of existing unsigned reconciler-authored events is in scope or out of scope; if out of scope, note that historical unsigned events represent a known pre-feature baseline and that `rebar verify-identity` output before vs. after this fix will differ for historical events.
findings.finding: The plan is silent on whether the existing corpus of unsigned reconciler-written events requires backfilling. AC1 implies forward-only (new events are verified), but this leaves a gap: if an operator runs `rebar verify-identity` store-wide today, all pre-fix reconciler events will appear as `unsigned`/`unknown-author`. The plan never explicitly declares 'no backfill is needed / out of scope', which is a migration-execution safety ambiguity under T11(e).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
